---
title: "Understanding the File Structure"
subtitle: "What each file does and how they work together"
---

## Overview

Now that your project is open in RStudio, you're probably looking at a list of files and
wondering what they all do. This chapter explains how a Quarto website is organized, what
each file's role is, and how they connect to produce the website you previewed in the
last chapter.

Understanding the structure before you start editing will save you a lot of confusion
later — so take a few minutes to read through this before making any changes.

---

## The Big Picture

A Quarto website is just a **folder of plain text files**. When you run `quarto preview`
or `quarto render`, Quarto reads those files and converts them into the HTML pages that
make up your website.

Here's a map of what's in your project folder and what each piece does:

```yaml
Personal_Website_Template/
│
├── _quarto.yml          ← The control center: site title, navigation, theme
├── index.qmd            ← Your homepage
├── research.qmd         ← Your research overview page
├── publications.qmd     ← Your publications list
├── projects.qmd         ← Your projects and models page
├── cv.qmd               ← Your CV page
├── modeling_basics.qmd  ← A learning/tutorial tab
│
├── custom.scss          ← Advanced style customization (colors, fonts)
├── styles.css           ← Additional styling rules
│
├── images/              ← Folder for photos and figures used on the site
├── files/               ← Folder for downloadable files like your CV PDF
├── docs/                ← Auto-generated folder; this is what gets published online
│
└── *.Rproj              ← RStudio project file (you opened this in Chapter 1)
```

::: {.callout-note}
You don't need to understand every file right now. Focus on the `.qmd` files and
`_quarto.yml` for now — those are where almost all of your editing will happen.
:::

---

## The `.qmd` Files — Your Page Content

Files ending in `.qmd` are **Quarto Markdown** files. Each one becomes a single page on
your website. They are plain text files, which means you can open and edit them in
RStudio like a document.

Here's what each page in your template is for:

| File | Page | What it contains |
|---|---|---|
| `index.qmd` | Homepage | Your name, photo, research summary, and featured tools |
| `research.qmd` | Research | An overview of your research areas and dissertation work |
| `publications.qmd` | Publications | A list of your papers, reports, and written work |
| `projects.qmd` | Projects / Models | Highlights of tools and projects you've built |
| `cv.qmd` | CV | A web version of your curriculum vitae |
| `modeling_basics.qmd` | Learning Tab | Educational content or resources you want to share |

If you open any of these files in RStudio, you'll see they follow the same basic pattern:
a short header block at the top (called the **YAML front matter**, enclosed in `---`),
followed by regular text with some formatting.

For example, at the top of `research.qmd` you'll see:
```yaml
---
title: "Research"
---
```

That's all Quarto needs to know the title of the page. Everything below that line is the
content that appears on the page.

::: {.callout-tip}
**What is Markdown?** Markdown is a simple way of formatting text using plain characters.
For example, putting `**text**` around a word makes it **bold**, and starting a line with
`##` makes it a heading. You don't need to learn all of Markdown right now — you'll pick
it up naturally as you edit the template pages.
:::

---

## The `_quarto.yml` File — Your Site's Control Center

The `_quarto.yml` file is the most important configuration file in your project. It
controls the overall structure of the site: the title, the navigation bar, the theme,
the footer, and more.

Open it in RStudio now and take a look. Here's what the key sections mean:

### Site Title
```yaml
website:
  title: "Vanessa Quintana"
```

This sets the name that appears in the browser tab and the top-left of the navigation
bar. You'll change this to your own name when you personalize the site.

### Navigation Bar
```yaml
navbar:
  left:
    - href: modeling_basics.qmd
      text: Modeling Basics
    - href: research.qmd
      text: Research
    - href: publications.qmd
      text: Publications
    - href: projects.qmd
      text: Models
    - href: cv.qmd
      text: CV
```

This is where the navigation bar links are defined. Each entry has two parts:
- `href` — the `.qmd` file this link points to
- `text` — the label that appears in the navbar

The order here controls the order the links appear on your site. If you want to rename a
tab or reorder the pages, this is where you do it.

### Social Links (Right Side of Navbar)
```yaml
    right:
      - icon: github
        href: https://github.com/vmahan1998
      - icon: envelope
        href: mailto:vanessa.mahan@maine.edu
      - icon: linkedin
        href: https://www.linkedin.com/in/vanessa-mahan/
```

These are the icon links that appear on the right side of the navigation bar. You'll
update these with your own GitHub, email, and LinkedIn in a later chapter.

### Theme
```yaml
format:
  html:
    theme: [flatly, custom.scss]
    css: styles.css
```

This tells Quarto which visual theme to use. The template uses **flatly** (a clean,
professional Bootstrap theme) combined with a custom style file (`custom.scss`) that
adds the dark color scheme you saw when you previewed the site.

### Output Folder
```yaml
project:
  output-dir: docs
```

This tells Quarto to save the built website files into a folder called `docs`. This
matters when it comes time to publish — GitHub Pages looks for your site files in the
`docs` folder by default.

::: {.callout-important}
Be careful editing `_quarto.yml`. It uses **YAML formatting**, which is very sensitive
to indentation. If a line is indented by the wrong number of spaces, Quarto may throw
an error. Always use **spaces** (not tabs) for indentation, and keep the existing
structure intact while making your edits.
:::

---

## The Styling Files

Your template has two files that control how the site looks visually:

**`styles.css`** — A CSS file that applies the dark background, text colors, card
styles, and other visual details across the site. CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) is the
standard language for styling web pages. You don't need to edit this file to personalize
your content, but you'll explore it in the styling chapter later.

**`custom.scss`** — A SCSS file that extends and customizes the base Quarto theme. SCSS
is a more powerful version of CSS. Again, you won't need to touch this right away.

::: {.callout-note}
The separation between content (`.qmd` files) and style (`styles.css`, `custom.scss`) is
intentional and useful. It means you can completely redesign the look of your site
without touching a single word of your content — and vice versa.
:::

---

## The `images/` and `files/` Folders

**`images/`** is where you store any photos or figures used on the site — your headshot,
research images, project screenshots, and so on. When a `.qmd` file references an image
like `images/Author_Picture.jpg`, Quarto looks for it in this folder.

**`files/`** is where you store downloadable files — most commonly a PDF version of your
CV. A link on your CV page points here so visitors can download it.

::: {.callout-tip}
When you're ready to personalize the site, you'll add your own headshot to the `images/`
folder and your CV PDF to the `files/` folder. You'll do this in Chapter 3.
:::

---

## The `docs/` Folder

The `docs/` folder is **automatically generated** by Quarto when you run
`quarto render`. You should never edit files inside `docs/` directly — Quarto will
overwrite your changes the next time it builds the site.

Think of `docs/` as the final, compiled version of your website. It's the folder that
gets published online when you deploy to GitHub Pages.

---

## Summary

| File / Folder | What it's for | Do you edit it? |
|---|---|---|
| `_quarto.yml` | Site structure, navigation, theme | Yes — for titles, links, settings |
| `*.qmd` files | Page content | Yes — your main editing happens here |
| `styles.css` | Visual styling | Later — for design customization |
| `custom.scss` | Theme customization | Later — for advanced styling |
| `images/` | Photos and figures | Yes — add your own images here |
| `files/` | Downloadable files (CV PDF) | Yes — add your CV PDF here |
| `docs/` | Auto-generated site output | Never — Quarto manages this |
| `*.Rproj` | RStudio project file | Never — leave this as-is |

Now that you understand how the pieces fit together, you're ready to start making the
site your own. In the next chapter, you'll edit your homepage and add your personal
information.
